result:
I want to talk about how utterly, ridiculously beautiful it is that we're all just temporary patterns of stardust arguing on the internet while hurling through space on a wet rock.

Yesterday I watched a spider rebuild its web at 3 a.m. while I couldn't sleep. The spider didn't know I existed. It didn't care that its entire life's work would be destroyed by morning cleaning or wind or some clumsy human. It just kept spinning. Eight legs moving like it was conducting an invisible orchestra. And I realized I've spent the last few years treating my own small creations (writing, relationships, projects) like they need to be permanent to matter.

They don't.

The spider taught me more about grace in one sleepless night than most self-help books ever have. Nothing lasts. The web gets wrecked. You build it again. Sometimes it's more beautiful the second time. Sometimes it's messier. Both are fine.

I've been thinking a lot about how we're all carrying around these invisible museums of every person we've ever loved, every version of ourselves we've outgrown, every stupid thing we said at 2 a.m. in 2009 that still makes us cringe. Our minds are just haunted houses and we're the reluctant tour guides.

But maybe that's the point.

The fact that I can sit here drinking terrible coffee, listening to lo-fi beats, and feel simultaneously:

- Ancient (because I remember when "buffering" was a way of life)
- Brand new (because every day still contains the possibility of meeting someone who rearranges my understanding of what's possible)
- Completely insignificant (in the grand scheme of the universe)
- Incredibly important (to the three people who would be devastated if I disappeared)

All of these things can be true at once. The human experience is basically just learning to hold more and more contradictory truths without losing your mind.

Anyway.

The sun is coming up where I am and it's doing that stupidly gorgeous pink-orange thing that makes me irrationally angry at how beautiful the world can be when I'm not even trying. There's a bird outside that's been singing the same four notes for twenty minutes like it's got something to prove.

I love this stupid planet.

I love that you're here reading whatever this is.

I love that neither of us will ever fully understand each other and yet here we are, two consciousnesses briefly overlapping in the digital ether.

Whatever you're carrying today, I hope you build your web anyway. Even if it gets destroyed. Especially if it gets destroyed.

The spider doesn't have existential dread. It just has silk.

Maybe we're supposed to be more like the spider.

Or maybe we're supposed to be exactly like this: messy, contradictory, awake at strange hours, writing to strangers about spiders.

Both are fine.

I'm glad you're alive, whoever you are.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
